title: Spain
subtitle: 1808–1900 · Peninsular War, Constitutions and the Long Crisis

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: A century of instability — 43 governments in 92 years

entries.label: Joseph I and Ferdinand VII — war and absolutism
entries.hint: Napoleon replaces the Bourbons with his brother Joseph I in 1808 — triggering the Peninsular War; Ferdinand VII returns in 1814 and immediately abolishes the 1812 Constitution; his decade-long absolutism, interrupted by the Trienio Liberal (1820–1823), ends with the First Carlist War breaking out at his death.
entries.note: Joseph I's appointment: Gaceta de Madrid 20 Jun 1808. Ferdinand VII's abolition of the 1812 Constitution: Royal Decree 4 May 1814, Gaceta de Madrid. The Trienio Liberal (1820–1823): Riego's pronunciamiento 1 Jan 1820 — documented in AGS. Carr (1982), Spain 1808–1975: the essential survey of the century. The 43 governments: counted in Solé Tura & Aja (1977), Constituciones y períodos constituyentes en España.

entries.label: Isabel II — the liberal monarchy
entries.hint: Isabel II reigns through two Carlist Wars, multiple constitutions, and 35 years of liberal-conservative oscillation (1833–1868) — she is overthrown by the Glorious Revolution of September 1868 and goes into exile; Spain then tries a constitutional monarchy with a foreign king, a republic, and a military coup before returning to the Bourbons.
entries.note: Isabel II's regency under her mother María Cristina (1833–1843): documented in Gaceta de Madrid. The Glorious Revolution 18 Sep 1868: press records and AGS. Burdiel (2010), Isabel II: the standard modern biography. The constitutional sequence: 1837, 1845, 1869 — all published in Gaceta de Madrid.

entries.label: The espadones — military brokers of Spanish liberalism
entries.hint: 19th-century Spanish politics is actually brokered by rival generals — Baldomero Espartero (Progressive, regent 1840–1843), Ramón María Narváez (Moderate), Leopoldo O'Donnell (Liberal Union); every change of political direction is driven by a military pronunciamiento, not a parliamentary election; the constitutions are real but the mechanism of power is the generals.
entries.note: Espartero's regency (1840–1843): documented in Gaceta de Madrid. His overthrow by Narváez 1843: ibid. O'Donnell's Liberal Union and his 1854 pronunciamiento (La Vicalvarada): documented. Comellas (1963), Los moderados en el poder 1844–1854: the standard study of the Narváez period. Bullough (2000), Spain: A History: contextualises the espadones. The pronounciamiento mechanism: every major political change from Riego (1820) to the Glorious Revolution (1868) is initiated by a military coup — documented in systematic form by Headrick (1981), Ejército y política en España.

entries.label: Alfonso XII and the Bourbon Restoration
entries.hint: Alfonso XII's restoration (1874) inaugurates the Cánovas turno pacífico — an arranged alternation of Liberal and Conservative parties that creates apparent political stability through managed elections (caciquismo); the system holds until 1898 when colonial disaster breaks it.
entries.note: Alfonso XII's Pronunciamiento of Sagunto 29 Dec 1874: Gaceta de Madrid. The Constitution of 1876: Gaceta de Madrid 30 Jun 1876. The turno pacífico system: Cánovas's design documented in his writings. Varela Ortega (1977), Los amigos políticos: the foundational study of caciquismo. Alfonso XII dies 1885; regency of María Cristina until 1902.

entries.label: First Spanish Republic — four presidents in eleven months
entries.hint: The First Spanish Republic lasts eleven months and has four presidents — it is torn apart by the Cantonal Revolution (July 1873) in which cities including Cartagena declare themselves sovereign cantons and fight the central government; General Pavía's coup on 3 January 1874 ends the republic; this chaos is the direct catalyst for the Bourbon Restoration (December 1874).
entries.note: The Republic's proclamation 11 Feb 1873: Gaceta de Madrid. The four presidents: Figueras, Pi i Margall, Salmerón, Castelar — all documented. The Cantonal Revolution: Cartagena holds out until January 1874 — the longest canton. Pavía's coup 3 Jan 1874: documented. The 1873 federal constitutional draft (never enacted): AHN Congreso. Hennessy (1962), The Federal Republic in Spain: the standard English study. Cartagena's last canton: documented in naval records (the cantonalists had seized warships).

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Peninsular War, Carlist Wars and colonial collapse

entries.label: Dos de Mayo — the uprising that begins the Peninsular War
entries.hint: The Madrid population rises against French occupation on 2 May 1808 — Murat's cavalry suppresses the uprising; executions follow on 3 May; Goya documents both days in two of the most powerful paintings in Western art; the national war begins the next day across Spain.
entries.note: Goya, The Second of May 1808 and The Third of May 1808 (painted c.1814): Prado (P000748, P000749). The uprising: documented in contemporary French military reports (Archives de la Guerre, Paris) and Spanish sources. Esdaile (2003), The Peninsular War: the standard modern study. The juntas that formed after 2 May: documented in AGS.

entries.label: Battle of Bailén — Napoleon's first European defeat
entries.hint: Spanish forces under General Castaños defeat and capture an entire French army corps at Bailén — Napoleon's first defeat in Europe; it shatters the myth of French invincibility and inspires resistance across Europe.
entries.note: Spanish military records: AGS Guerra. French records: Archives de la Guerre, Paris — Dupont's surrender documents. Contemporary impact across Europe: documented in press records (Times of London, 23 Aug 1808). Esdaile (2003), Peninsular War, ch.3. The 18,000 French prisoners: their fate documented in AGS.

entries.label: Rafael del Riego and the Trienio Liberal — liberalism's first test
entries.loc: Las Cabezas de San Juan (pronunciamiento); Madrid; Cádiz
entries.hint: Colonel Rafael del Riego pronounces at Las Cabezas de San Juan on 1 January 1820 — forcing Ferdinand VII to swear the 1812 Constitution; the resulting Trienio Liberal (1820–1823) produces Spain's first Penal Code (1822), directly derails the reconquest of the American colonies (Riego's army was gathered for that purpose), and ends when France invades to restore absolutism.
entries.note: Riego's pronunciamiento 1 Jan 1820: documented in press and official records. Ferdinand VII's oath to the 1812 Constitution: Gaceta de Madrid 9 Mar 1820. The Trienio Liberal: Gaceta de Madrid (official source throughout). The Penal Code of 1822: Gaceta de Madrid — Spain's first systematic criminal code. Fontana (1979), La crisis del Antiguo Régimen 1808–1833: the standard study. Riego is executed 7 Nov 1823 after French intervention — documented. His march (*Himno de Riego*) becomes the anthem of Spanish liberalism and later of the Second Republic.

entries.label: Hundred Thousand Sons of Saint Louis — France restores absolutism
entries.hint: The Holy Alliance authorises France to invade Spain and crush the Trienio Liberal — the Duc d'Angoulême leads 100,000 troops across the Pyrenees on 7 April 1823; they reach Madrid in weeks; the liberal government retreats to Cádiz; Ferdinand VII is freed and immediately abrogates all Trienio legislation; the brutal Ominous Decade (1823–1833) follows.
entries.note: The invasion order: Congress of Verona (1822) — documented in the Congress minutes. French military records: Archives de la Guerre, Vincennes. The Duc d'Angoulême's campaign: documented in French and Spanish sources. Ferdinand VII's Valenciazo (royal decree annulling Trienio legislation, 1 Oct 1823): Gaceta de Madrid. Fontana (1979). The Ominous Decade's repression: documented in AHN. This is how Spain's first liberal experiment ends — by foreign military intervention.
keywords: Hundred Thousand Sons of Saint Louis

entries.label: First Carlist War — dynastic civil war
entries.hint: On Ferdinand VII's death, his brother Carlos claims the throne against his daughter Isabel — triggering a seven-year civil war fought primarily in the Basque Country and Navarre; the Carlists represent traditionalism (Church, fueros, monarchy); the Liberals represent constitutional modernisation.
entries.note: The war's military records: AGS Guerra Moderna. The Convention of Vergara (31 Aug 1839): the peace agreement — text in Gaceta de Madrid 1 Sep 1839. Holt (1967), The Carlist Wars in Spain: the standard English study. Three Carlist Wars in total (1833–1840, 1846–1849, 1872–1876) make 19th-century Spain's civil conflict the longest in Western European history.

entries.label: Second Carlist War (1846–1849) — matrinat revolt in Catalonia
entries.hint: The Second Carlist War (1846–1849) is fought primarily in Catalonia — the Montemolinists (supporters of Carlos VI) wage guerrilla warfare before Isabel II's forces suppress them; shorter and less intense than the First, it nevertheless confirms that the dynastic-ideological split between liberal and traditionalist Spain has not been resolved by military defeat.
entries.note: Military operations documented in AGS Guerra. Holt (1967), The Carlist Wars in Spain: the standard English study covering all three wars. Bullough (2000), Spain: A History: contextualises the Second Carlist War within the dynastic conflict. The peace of Vergara (1839) that ended the First Carlist War had not resolved the underlying legitimacy dispute — documented in the political press of the 1840s (BNE Hemeroteca).

entries.label: Moroccan War (1859–1860) — O'Donnell's prestige campaign
entries.hint: General O'Donnell launches a war against Morocco ostensibly over border incidents at Ceuta — the Battle of Tetuán (4 February 1860) is Spain's first major military victory in decades; the war boosts national pride and O'Donnell's Liberal Union government; Spain gains Tetuán briefly and an expanded Ceuta; it briefly unites a divided country behind a foreign adventure.
entries.note: The Battle of Tetuán 4 Feb 1860: AGS Guerra. The Treaty of Wad-Ras 26 Apr 1860: AHN — Spain gains Tetuán (returned 1862) and an enlarged Ceuta. Alarcón, Diario de un testigo de la guerra de África (1860): primary eyewitness account — BNE. Morales Lezcano (1976), El colonialismo hispanofrancés en Marruecos: the standard study. The war's domestic political function: documented in O'Donnell's correspondence (AHN).

entries.label: Ten Years War — Cuba's first independence war (1868–1878)
entries.loc: Cuba (Oriente province; Yara — Grito de Yara)
entries.hint: The Ten Years War begins with Carlos Manuel de Céspedes's Grito de Yara (10 October 1868) — he frees his slaves and leads an uprising; 200,000 die over ten years; the Pact of Zanjón (1878) ends hostilities with promises of reform that are not kept; the unresolved grievances directly fuel the 1895 war; the Ten Years War is the first chapter of Cuba's independence story.
entries.note: The Grito de Yara 10 Oct 1868: documented in Cuban and Spanish press. The Pact of Zanjón 10 Feb 1878: AHN Estado — the primary document. Ferrer (1999), Insurgent Cuba: Race, Nation and Revolution 1868–1898: the standard modern study. Thomas (1971), Cuba: the Pursuit of Freedom, ch.10–14: the standard narrative history. The death toll estimate (200,000): documented in Spanish military records (AGS) and contemporary Cuban sources.
keywords: Carlos Manuel de Céspedes Grito de Yara

entries.label: Third Carlist War (1872–1876) — the last Carlist stand
entries.hint: The Third Carlist War (1872–1876) coincides with the First Republic's chaos and the Cantonal Revolution — Carlos VII comes close to taking Madrid; Cánovas's Restoration of Alfonso XII in 1874 consolidates the liberal state; the military defeat at Montejurra (February 1876) ends organised Carlism as a military force; the Basque and Navarrese fueros are abolished as punishment.
entries.note: The Third Carlist War: AGS Guerra. The abolition of the Basque fueros (Ley de 21 Jul 1876): BOE — direct consequence of Carlist defeat. Holt (1967): the standard account. The connection between Carlist defeat and Basque nationalism: Arana founds the PNV in 1895 partly as a response to fueros abolition — documented. The Cantonal Revolution's simultaneous chaos: documented in Hennessy (1962), The Federal Republic in Spain.

entries.label: Disaster of '98 — Cuba, Puerto Rico and the Philippines lost
entries.hint: Spain is defeated by the United States in 10 weeks — Cuba, Puerto Rico and the Philippines are lost; Spain's last remnants of empire are gone; the 'Generation of '98' intellectual movement responds with a crisis of national identity that shapes the entire 20th century.
entries.note: Treaty of Paris 10 Dec 1898: US Senate ratification records; Spanish text in Gaceta de Madrid. The naval battles: US Navy records (NARA) and Spanish AGS Marina. Tone (2006), War and Genocide in Cuba 1895–1898. The Spanish navy's losses at Santiago de Cuba 3 Jul 1898: documented in AGS Marina — complete destruction of the Caribbean fleet. This entry bridges directly to the spain-1900-1975 folio.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Spain's constitutional laboratory — six constitutions in one century

entries.label: Constitution of 1812 — La Pepa
entries.hint: The Cádiz Constitution is proclaimed on Saint Joseph's Day (hence 'La Pepa') — the first Spanish liberal constitution; it establishes national sovereignty, abolishes the Inquisition, ends feudal privileges, and provides for freedom of the press; it becomes the model for liberal constitutions across southern Europe and Latin America.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Original text in Archivo de las Cortes (Madrid) and Archivo Municipal de Cádiz. Published in Gaceta de la Regencia de España e Indias, 19 Mar 1812. Fernández Sarasola (2002), La Constitución de Cádiz: the standard modern study. The constitution's international influence: explicitly cited in the Portuguese (1822), Italian (1820) and Norwegian (1814) constitutions.

entries.label: Five constitutions — Spain's 19th-century constitutional instability
entries.hint: Spain enacts five constitutions in one century — 1812, 1837, 1845, 1869, 1876; the 1834 Estatuto Real was a royal charter, not a full constitution; the 1873 federal republican draft was never enacted due to the Cantonal Revolution and General Pavía's coup; no constitution lasts more than 30 years; the instability is itself the primary historical fact.
entries.note: All six texts: Gaceta de Madrid (each promulgation date). Solé Tura & Aja (1977), Constituciones y períodos constituyentes en España: the standard comparative study. The 1869 Constitution (the most liberal, granting universal male suffrage, religious freedom): Gaceta de Madrid 6 Jun 1869. The 1876 Constitution (the Restoration settlement, lasting to 1931): Gaceta de Madrid 30 Jun 1876.

entries.label: Abolition of señoríos (1837) — feudal jurisdiction ended
entries.hint: The liberal governments of the 1830s–1840s abolish the señoríos jurisdiccionales — the feudal rights of noble and ecclesiastical lords to exercise justice, collect tolls and levy dues over their territories; combined with the desamortización, it creates the legal framework of modern property rights; but the land itself mostly stays with existing large owners, creating the latifundio problem.
entries.note: Decree abolishing señoríos jurisdiccionales 1837: Gaceta de Madrid. The 1811 Cortes of Cádiz decree (partially implemented): the earlier step. García Ormaechea (1932), Supervivencias feudales en España: the foundational study. Fontana (1979), La crisis del Antiguo Régimen: contextualises abolition within the liberal revolution. The practical gap: abolishing jurisdiction ≠ redistributing land — the same families often retained ownership, documented in cadastral records.

entries.label: Ley Moyano (1857) — the foundation of Spanish public education
entries.hint: The Ley de Instrucción Pública (Moyano, 9 September 1857) establishes Spain's first systematic public education framework — primary education mandatory and free for the poor; a three-tier structure (primary, secondary, university); it is Spain's most durable 19th-century legislation, structuring education until 1970; its implementation is chronically underfunded.
entries.note: Ley Moyano 9 Sep 1857: BOE — primary document. Ministerio de Educación historical archive. Puelles Benítez (1980), Educación e ideología en la España contemporánea: the standard study. The implementation gap: documented in INE literacy statistics (1860 census: 75% illiteracy rate despite the law). The Institución Libre de Enseñanza (1876): founded partly as a reaction to the Moyano law's clerical influence — connection documented.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Church, state and the religious question

entries.label: Abolition of the Spanish Inquisition — 1834
entries.hint: The Inquisition is abolished for the third and final time on 15 July 1834 — it had been abolished by Joseph Bonaparte (1808), restored by Ferdinand VII (1814), and finally suppressed definitively; its 356 years of operation are formally ended by a single royal decree.
entries.note: Royal Decree 15 Jul 1834: Gaceta de Madrid 16 Jul 1834. The abolitions and restorations documented in: 1808 (Joseph I): Gaceta de Madrid; 1813 (Cádiz Cortes): ibid.; 1814 (Ferdinand VII): ibid.; 1820 (Trienio): ibid.; 1823 (restored): ibid.; 1834 (final): ibid. The entire cycle is documented in consecutive Gaceta issues — a remarkable primary source for constitutional instability.

entries.label: Ecclesiastical confiscations — Mendizábal's desamortización
entries.hint: Prime Minister Mendizábal seizes and sells Church and monastic properties — approximately 100,000 properties auctioned; the measure finances the First Carlist War but destroys the monastic system and creates a new landowning class; the single most significant redistribution of wealth in 19th-century Spain.
entries.note: Mendizábal's decree: Gaceta de Madrid 19 Feb 1836. The confiscation records: AHN Clero (the primary archive — the most detailed surviving record of pre-industrial Spanish Church property). Tomás y Valiente (1971), El marco político de la desamortización en España: the foundational study. The 100,000 properties figure: derived from AHN Clero auction records.

entries.label: Concordat of 1851 — Church and State reconcile after desamortización
entries.hint: The Concordat of 1851 reconciles the Spanish Church with the liberal state after the Mendizábal desamortización — the Vatican accepts the confiscations as irreversible in exchange for state funding of clergy salaries and Catholic control of education; it is the legal architecture of Church-State relations for the rest of the century and the model that Francoism later inverts with its own 1953 Concordat.
entries.note: Concordat 16 Mar 1851: BOE — primary document. Negotiated by Bravo Murillo. Callahan (2000), The Catholic Church in Spain 1875–1998, ch.2: the standard study. The state funding clause: Article 33 — the state pays clergy salaries in compensation for confiscated properties. The education clause: Article 2 — Catholic doctrine taught in all public schools. The Concordat's longevity: it governs Church-State relations until partial renegotiation under Franco (1953).

entries.label: Religious freedom (1869) and the Protestant revival
entries.loc: Spain (nationwide; main communities in Andalusia and Madrid)
entries.hint: Article 21 of the 1869 Constitution guarantees religious freedom for the first time in Spanish history — Protestant missionaries (primarily British) immediately establish churches in Madrid, Seville and across Andalusia; the Protestant revival is brief (the 1876 Restoration Constitution narrows freedoms again) but it produces Spain's first minority religious press and the first non-Catholic civil marriages.
entries.note: Constitution of 1869, Article 21: AHN Congreso — primary. The Protestant missions: documented in the archives of the British and Foreign Bible Society (Cambridge). Vilar (1994), Un mundo desconocido: los protestantes españoles: the standard study. The 1876 Restoration Constitution Article 11: tolerates private practice only — documented. The non-Catholic civil marriage records: Registro Civil (from 1870) — AHN.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Goya's Disasters, Romanticism and the realist novel

entries.label: Goya: Disasters of War — eyewitness atrocity
entries.hint: Goya's 82 etching series documents the atrocities of the Peninsular War — torture, massacre, famine, execution; printed only posthumously in 1863; they are the most powerful visual testimony of 19th-century warfare and the first anti-war art.
entries.note: Original copper plates: Real Academia de Bellas Artes de San Fernando, Madrid. First printed edition 1863 (posthumous): Biblioteca Nacional de España. Goya made them during or just after the war — their sequence and dating studied by Licht (1979). Hughes (2003), Goya, ch.12–14. The plates were never published in Goya's lifetime — possibly too dangerous under Ferdinand VII's absolutism.

entries.label: Gustavo Adolfo Bécquer — the defining voice of Spanish Romanticism
entries.hint: Gustavo Adolfo Bécquer's Rimas (1871, published posthumously by his friends) are the most widely read Spanish poems of the 19th century — spare, melancholic quatrains on love, poetry and death; his Leyendas are the defining Romantic prose tales; he dies at 34 in Madrid poverty; his collected works are published by subscription within months of his death, documenting immediate canonisation.
entries.note: Rimas y Leyendas (1871): first edition edited by friends — BNE (R/7961). The manuscript Libro de los gorriones (the poet's own notebook): BNE Ms.17669 — primary autograph source. Pageard (1990), Bécquer: Leyenda y realidad: the standard biography. The subscription publication: documented in contemporary press (La Ilustración de Madrid, Dec 1870). His influence: cited by virtually every Spanish poet of the 20th century as the primary Romantic predecessor — documented in Lorca, Machado and Jiménez.

entries.label: Benito Pérez Galdós — Episodios Nacionales
entries.hint: Galdós writes 46 historical novels covering Spain from Trafalgar (1805) to the Restoration — the most ambitious literary history of a century in any European language; his novels are simultaneously primary sources for social history, drawing on direct research in archives and personal memory.
entries.note: First series (Trafalgar, 1873): BNE first edition. Galdós's research methods: documented in his notebooks (Biblioteca Nacional de España, Archivo Galdós). Berkowitz (1948), Pérez Galdós: Spanish Liberal Crusader: the standard biography. The Episodios Nacionales as historical source: Bly (1983) analyses their archival basis. Note: Galdós (1843–1920) straddles both the 1808–1900 and 1900–1975 folios.

entries.label: Antoni Gaudí and Catalan Modernisme — the Sagrada Família begun
entries.loc: Barcelona (Sagrada Família; Casa Batlló; Güell Park)
entries.hint: Antoni Gaudí takes over the Sagrada Família in 1883 and transforms it into the most extraordinary religious building of the modern era; alongside Domènech i Montaner and Puig i Cadafalch, he creates Catalan Modernisme — a synthesis of Gothic structure, Moorish decoration, Art Nouveau and Catalan nationalism that is Spain's most internationally celebrated artistic contribution.
entries.note: Sagrada Família: Gaudí takes over from Francisco de Paula del Villar in 1883 — Arxiu de la Junta Constructora del Temple Expiatori de la Sagrada Família. UNESCO World Heritage (2005). Gaudí's building accounts: Arxiu de la Junta. Domènech i Montaner, Palau de la Música Catalana (1908): UNESCO 1997. Puig i Cadafalch, Casa Amatller (1900): extant. Bassegoda i Nonell (2000), El gran Gaudí: the standard Catalan study. Gaudí dies 10 June 1926 — knocked down by a tram; identified only because he had no money in his pockets; documented in press.

entries.label: La Verbena de la Paloma — zarzuela and popular theatre
entries.hint: Tomás Bretón's La Verbena de la Paloma (1894) is the masterpiece of the zarzuela — Spanish musical theatre combining spoken dialogue and song; its setting in working-class Madrid on the Feast of La Paloma (15 August) makes it the most detailed musical document of late 19th-century popular urban life.
entries.note: First performance Teatro Apolo, Madrid, 17 Feb 1894: documented in press records and theatre archives. Bretón's manuscript score: Sociedad General de Autores y Editores (SGAE), Madrid. Contemporary reviews: La Época, El Imparcial (Madrid press archive). Cotarelo y Mori (1934), Historia de la zarzuela: the foundational study. The libretto (Ricardo de la Vega): BNE.
keywords: Tomás Bretón La Verbena de la Paloma

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Liberalism, Krausism and the roots of 20th-century Spanish thought

entries.label: Krausism — the philosophical engine of Spanish liberalism
entries.hint: Julián Sanz del Río introduces Karl Christian Friedrich Krause's philosophy to Spain in 1854 — its organic, harmonious pantheism and secular ethics become the philosophical engine of Spanish liberalism; Krausism directly inspires the Institución Libre de Enseñanza and triggers the bitter Cuestión Universitaria academic freedom battles (1875) when conservative professors are expelled for refusing Krausist tolerance.
entries.note: Sanz del Río, Ideal de la humanidad para la vida (1860): BNE — his adaptation of Krause. The Cuestión Universitaria (1875): documented in the Gaceta de Madrid and university records — 11 professors including Giner de los Ríos expelled for refusing to abandon Krausist principles. López Morillas (1956), El krausismo español: the standard study. The ILE connection: Giner de los Ríos (the ILE's founder) is Sanz del Río's direct disciple — documented. Cacho Viu (1962), La Institución Libre de Enseñanza: documents the Krausism-ILE lineage.

entries.label: Pi i Margall — the theory of Spanish federalism
entries.hint: Francesc Pi i Margall's La reacción y la revolución (1854) is the founding text of Spanish federal democratic thought — arguing for a bottom-up pact between sovereign municipalities and regions; as President of the First Republic (June–July 1873) he attempts to implement federalism but the Cantonal Revolution overtakes him; his thought directly feeds 20th-century Catalan and regional autonomy movements.
entries.note: La reacción y la revolución (1854): BNE — primary. Las nacionalidades (1877): his mature federalist theory — BNE. His presidential tenure (11 Jun–18 Jul 1873): Gaceta de la República. Jutglar (1975), Pi y Margall y el federalismo español: the standard study. The connection to 20th-century regionalism: explicitly acknowledged in the 1931 Republican Constitution debates — Diario de Sesiones. His thought as the intellectual foundation of the 1978 Estado de las Autonomías: documented in the constitutional debates.

entries.label: Institución Libre de Enseñanza — secular education
entries.hint: Francisco Giner de los Ríos founds the Free Teaching Institution after being expelled from the University of Madrid for refusing to conform his teaching to Church and Crown orthodoxy — its graduates include García Lorca, Machado and Ortega y Gasset; it is the intellectual nursery of the Second Republic.
entries.note: Foundation act 1876: BILE (Boletín de la Institución Libre de Enseñanza) — the institution's own journal, published 1877–1936, BNE. Giner de los Ríos's expulsion from the university: documented in university records. Jiménez-Landi (1973–1996), La Institución Libre de Enseñanza (4 vols): the standard study. The ILE's alumni: documented in its membership records (BILE).

entries.label: Menéndez Pelayo — conservative Catholic intellectual Spain
entries.loc: Santander (born 1856); Madrid (Real Academia Española; BNE director)
entries.hint: Marcelino Menéndez Pelayo's Historia de los heterodoxos españoles (1880–1882) is the defining statement of traditionalist intellectual Spain — a vast catalogue of Spanish heretics, freethinkers and unorthodox thinkers framed as deviations from the authentic Catholic essence of the nation; it is the primary intellectual monument of conservative thought and the direct counterweight to Krausism.
entries.note: Historia de los heterodoxos españoles (3 vols, 1880–1882): BNE — primary. His Historia de las ideas estéticas en España (1883–1891): the standard history of Spanish aesthetics. Director of the Biblioteca Nacional 1898–1912: BNE institutional records. Real Academia Española membership: RAE records. Laín Entralgo (1956), Menéndez Pelayo: the standard intellectual biography. The Heterodoxos as a polemical text: explicitly written against Krausism — documented in his correspondence (Biblioteca de Menéndez Pelayo, Santander).

entries.label: Generation of '98 — the intellectual response to disaster
entries.hint: The '98 disaster produces a generation of writers who interrogate Spanish identity and national failure — Unamuno, Azorín, Baroja, Machado, Valle-Inclán; their essays, novels and poetry are primary evidence for elite self-questioning at the century's end.
entries.note: The term 'Generation of '98': coined by Azorín in a 1913 article — documented in press archive. Unamuno, En torno al casticismo (1895): BNE. Azorín, El alma castellana (1900): BNE. Fox (1997), Reimagining Spain: the standard English study. The generation's diverse politics (ranging from Unamuno's Catholic nationalism to Baroja's anarchism) is documented in their own correspondence.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Economic backwardness, industrialisation and the agrarian question

entries.label: Río Tinto mines — British capital and industrial mining (1873)
entries.hint: The Rio Tinto Company Limited (British) acquires the ancient copper and sulphur mines at Huelva in 1873 for £3.5 million — introducing industrial-scale hydraulic mining, steam engines and a private railway to the port; by 1890 Río Tinto is the world's largest copper producer; the 1888 'Año de los Tiros' massacre (workers protest sulphur fumes, troops fire into the crowd) is documented.
entries.note: Rio Tinto Company purchase 1873: Companies House, UK (registered 29 Mar 1873). The Año de los Tiros (4 Feb 1888): documented in local and national press (BNE Hemeroteca) and in the Company records (Rio Tinto archive, now at University of Huelva). Harvey (1981), The Rio Tinto Company: An Economic History: the standard study using the company archive. The railway (Huelva to mines, 1875): documented in Ministerio de Fomento railway records.

entries.label: Agrarian crisis and caciquismo — the Andalusian question
entries.hint: The desamortización created a class of large landowners (latifundistas) in the south rather than small farmers — by 1900 Andalusia has the most unequal land distribution in Europe; seasonal day labourers (jornaleros) live in poverty; anarchism takes root.
entries.note: Land distribution data: Ministerio de Hacienda amillaramiento records (1860s–1880s): AHN. The jornalero condition: Costa (1902), Oligarquía y caciquismo — a primary source document based on a survey of local conditions. Bernal (1988), La lucha por la tierra en la crisis del Antiguo Régimen: the standard study. The anarchist movement's roots in Andalusian agrarian conditions: Temma Kaplan (1977), Anarchists of Andalusia.

entries.label: Arancel Cánovas (1891) — the tariff that strangled Spain's colonies
entries.hint: The ultra-protectionist Arancel Cánovas (1891) closes Spain's market to foreign grain and industrial imports — satisfying Castile's wheat barons, Catalan textile magnates and Basque steel producers at the cost of raising food prices for the poor and forcing Cuba and the Philippines to buy expensive peninsular goods; the colonial resentment it generates directly fuels the final independence wars.
entries.note: Arancel de Aduanas 1891: Gaceta de Madrid. The political coalition behind it: Castilian wheat, Catalan textile, Basque iron — documented in parliamentary debates (Diario de Sesiones). The colonial impact: Cubans paying peninsular prices for goods they could buy cheaper from the US — documented in Cuban commercial records. Fradera (1999), Gobernar colonias: the standard study of the colonial-metropolis economic relationship. Tortella (2000), The Development of Modern Spain ch.4: quantifies the tariff's economic effects.

entries.label: Catalan industrialisation — the cotton revolution
entries.hint: Catalonia industrialises around cotton textiles from the 1830s — by 1900 it has 80% of Spain's industrial capacity; the contrast between industrial Catalonia and agrarian Andalusia shapes Spanish politics for the next century.
entries.note: Factory census records: Archivo Municipal de Barcelona. The 1856 Junta de Comercio census: BNE. Nadal (1975), El fracaso de la Revolución Industrial en España: the foundational economic history of Spanish industrialisation's limits. The concentration in Catalonia: documented in National Census records (INE historical series). The contrast with the south: described in contemporary travel literature and social investigation.

entries.label: Electric telegraph network (1855) — Spain's first national information grid
entries.hint: Spain's electric telegraph network is built simultaneously with the railways under the 1855 infrastructure laws — by 1860 the network connects all provincial capitals; it transforms the speed of political and military communication; Cánovas's ministers use it to orchestrate the Bourbon Restoration (December 1874) in real time across the peninsula.
entries.note: Ley de Telégrafos 22 Apr 1855: BOE — primary. The network expansion statistics: Ministerio de Gobernación telegraph reports (AHN). Bahamonde, Martínez Lorente and Otero (1993), Las comunicaciones en la construcción del Estado contemporáneo en España: the standard study. The Restoration telegraph operation (29 Dec 1874): Cánovas's correspondence documented in AHN — the pronunciamiento coordinated by telegraph is a primary case study in new media and politics.

entries.label: Ildefons Cerdà — the Eixample plan and the science of urbanism
entries.hint: Ildefons Cerdà's 1859 plan for Barcelona's Eixample (Extension) is the founding document of modern urbanism — a scientific grid of octagonal blocks designed for light, ventilation and social equality, with data on housing conditions, mortality rates and street widths; Cerdà coins the word 'urbanización' (urbanisation); the Eixample remains the world's largest intact example of rational city planning.
entries.note: Cerdà, Teoría General de la Urbanización (1867): BNE — the primary theoretical text. The 1859 plan: Arxiu Històric de la Ciutat de Barcelona (AHCB). UNESCO recognition of Cerdà's contribution: documented. Soria y Puig (1999), Cerdà: The Five Bases of the General Theory of Urbanization: the standard English edition. The mortality data Cerdà used: his Monografía estadística de la clase obrera de Barcelona (1856) — the primary social investigation.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Colonial independence, international isolation and the Atlantic world

entries.label: Spanish American independence — the empire dissolves
entries.hint: Spain loses virtually all its American empire between 1810 and 1826 — 18 new republics emerge; 300 years of colonial rule ends; Spain is left with Cuba, Puerto Rico and the Philippines; 300,000 Spanish soldiers die in the independence wars.
entries.note: Bolívar's Carta de Jamaica (6 Sep 1815): the most important programmatic document of Latin American independence — original at Archivo Nacional de Colombia. The Spanish military records: AGS Guerra. Lynch (1973), The Spanish American Revolutions: the standard study. The economic consequence for Spain: Tortella (2000), The Development of Modern Spain, ch.6.

entries.label: Cuban sugar and tobacco — Spain's colonial trade lifeline
entries.hint: Cuba is Spain's most profitable remaining colony — by 1850 Cuba produces one-third of the world's sugar using enslaved labour; Spanish merchants in Cádiz and Catalan textile manufacturers in Barcelona both depend on the Cuban market; the tariff system forcing Cuba to buy expensive Spanish goods directly fuels independence sentiment; losing Cuba in 1898 is an economic as much as a political catastrophe.
entries.note: Cuban trade statistics: Junta de Fomento de Cuba records (Archivo Nacional de Cuba) and Spanish customs data (AHN). The one-third of world sugar production: documented in Humboldt (1826), Ensayo político sobre la isla de Cuba — primary. Fradera (1999), Gobernar colonias: the standard study of the colonial trade relationship. The protectionist tariff and Cuban resentment: documented in Cuban creole press and Spanish parliamentary debates.

entries.label: Railway Law of 1855 — wider gauge isolates Spain from Europe
entries.hint: The Ley de Ferrocarriles (1855) mandates a rail gauge 23cm wider than the European standard (1,668mm vs 1,435mm) — a decision shaped by engineering miscalculation and military caution; French capital finances the construction; the wider gauge physically isolates Spain's commercial exchange from the rest of Europe for over a century, requiring goods to be transferred at the border.
entries.note: Ley de Ferrocarriles 3 Jun 1855: Gaceta de Madrid. The gauge decision: debated in the Comisión de Ingenieros 1844 report — AHN. The French capital: Pérès-Brémond (2000) on French railway investment in Spain. Tedde (1978), Las compañías ferroviarias en España 1855–1935: the standard study. The border transfer problem: documented in customs records. The gauge difference is finally being resolved with high-speed AVE lines using standard gauge — the 19th-century decision still shaping 21st-century infrastructure.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Spain's 19th-century international isolation and colonial twilight

entries.label: Spain as Europe's 'sick man of the century'
entries.hint: Throughout the 19th century Spain is marginalised from the main currents of European development — absent from the Concert of Europe, late to industrialise, riven by civil wars; its European neighbours cease to treat it as a major power; the contrast with France (Napoleonic, industrial) and Britain (imperial, industrial) is stark.
entries.note: Spain's absence from the Concert of Europe: Kissinger (1957), A World Restored — Spain not mentioned as a Great Power. The contrast in industrial output: Mitchell (1975), European Historical Statistics — Spain's industrialisation lag documented. The 'sick man' characterisation: contemporaries including Marx (articles on Spain 1854: New-York Tribune) used this framing. Álvarez Junco (2001), Mater Dolorosa: analyses Spain's 19th-century national identity crisis.

entries.label: Congress of Vienna (1815) — Spain sidelined despite the Peninsular War
entries.hint: Despite Spain's massive sacrifices in the Peninsular War (which Wellington himself called the decisive theatre), Spain is entirely sidelined at the Congress of Vienna — receiving zero territorial or financial compensation; the Congress cements Spain's demotion to a second-class European power, a status that shapes its entire 19th-century political isolation.
entries.note: Congress of Vienna Final Act 9 Jun 1815: the standard text — no Spanish territorial gains. Spain's delegation: led by Pedro Gómez Labrador — documented as ineffective in diplomatic records. Kissinger (1957), A World Restored: analyses Spain's marginalisation. The contrast with the scale of Spanish sacrifice (Peninsular War 1808–1814): documented in military records. Álvarez Junco (2001), Mater Dolorosa: analyses the Vienna marginalisation's impact on Spanish national identity.

entries.label: US intervention and the Treaty of Paris (1898) — empire ends
entries.hint: The United States declares war on Spain on 25 April 1898 — the Spanish-American War lasts 109 days; the US destroys the Spanish fleets at Manila Bay (1 May) and Santiago de Cuba (3 July); the Treaty of Paris (10 December 1898) transfers Cuba, Puerto Rico, Guam and the Philippines to US control for $20 million; 400 years of Spanish empire in the Americas and Asia end in four months.
entries.note: US declaration of war 25 Apr 1898: US Congressional Record. Treaty of Paris 10 Dec 1898: US National Archives and AHN — primary. The Battle of Manila Bay 1 May 1898: documented in US Navy records. Tone (2006), War and Genocide in Cuba 1895–1898: the standard modern study. Foner (1972), The Spanish-Cuban-American War (2 vols): the definitive history. The $20 million payment: Treaty text Article III.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Concepción Arenal — criminologist and feminist
entries.hint: Concepción Arenal attends university disguised as a man, becomes Spain's first female criminologist, and writes the foundational texts of Spanish prison reform and social feminism — her La mujer del porvenir (1869) is the most important 19th-century Spanish feminist text.
entries.note: La mujer del porvenir (1869): BNE first edition. Her disguise to attend university: documented in her biographies and her own later accounts. Her reports on prison conditions: Ministerio de Justicia records (AHN). Lacalzada de Mateo (1994), Mentalidad y proyección civil de Concepción Arenal: the standard biography. Her role as Visitadora de Cárceles (prison inspector): official appointment in AHN.

entries.label: Penal Codes 1822 and 1848 — the decriminalisation of same-sex acts
entries.hint: The actual decriminalisation of consensual same-sex acts in Spain occurs in the Penal Code of 1822 (Trienio Liberal) — the first time sodomy is omitted from criminal statutes; the Penal Code of 1848 (under Isabel II) permanently consolidates this omission; the 1870 Code simply maintains it; the CATEGORICAL SHIFT is 1822, not 1870.
entries.note: Penal Code of 1822 (first omission of sodomy): enacted during the Trienio Liberal — Gaceta de Madrid 1822. This is the actual CATEGORICAL SHIFT. The 1848 Code (and 1850 revision): permanently consolidates the omission — Gaceta de Madrid. The 1870 Liberal Penal Code: maintains the established absence — Gaceta de Madrid 31 Aug 1870. Ugarte Pérez (2008), Una discriminación universal: the standard study — documents the 1822 shift as the key moment. Spain's decriminalisation predates France's 1791 decriminalisation in legal form but the 1822 Code is the operative Spanish precedent.

entries.label: Quintos — conscription of the poor and the blood tax
entries.loc: Spain (nationwide; colonial wars in Cuba and Morocco)
entries.hint: The quintas (military conscription) system allows wealthy families to pay a redemption fee (redención en metálico) to exempt their sons — only the poorest young men fight and die in disease-ridden colonial wars in Cuba, Morocco and the Philippines; the 1895 Cuban call-up sends 200,000 men, of whom 60,000+ die mainly of yellow fever; anti-conscription riots repeatedly shake Spanish cities.
entries.note: The redención en metálico system: documented in conscription laws (Gaceta de Madrid). The Cuba casualties (1895–1898): 60,000+ deaths documented — mainly disease; AGS Guerra. The 1896 Barcelona anti-conscription riots: Arxiu Municipal de Barcelona. Cardona (1983), El Poder Militar en la España contemporánea: the standard study. The asymmetry: wealthy families documented as consistently buying exemptions — notarial records. The quintas system's injustice: explicitly identified by Concepción Arenal and other 19th-century reformers as a primary social grievance.

entries.label: Jornaleros — seasonal starvation and the roots of Andalusian anarchism
entries.loc: Andalusia (Cádiz; Córdoba; Sevilla provinces — latifundio zones)
entries.hint: The desamortización concentrates southern land in latifundista hands — the landless day-labourers (jornaleros) face systematic starvation during the winter 'dead season' when there is no agricultural work; the gap between harvest labour and winter survival is documented in social investigations; this structural hunger directly drives the rapid spread of anarchism and agrarian revolts across Andalusia.
entries.note: Costa, Oligarquía y caciquismo (1902): primary survey documenting jornalero conditions. Bernal (1988), La lucha por la tierra: the standard study using estate and municipal records. The Jerez uprising 1892 (jornaleros attempt to seize the town): documented in press and military records. Temma Kaplan (1977), Anarchists of Andalusia: uses municipal archives to document the famine-anarchism connection. The INE historical census: documents seasonal migration patterns and mortality rates. The gap between harvest wages and winter subsistence: documented in contemporary economic surveys.

entries.label: Barcelona general strike (1855) — Spain's first
entries.hint: On 2 July 1855 Barcelona's textile workers launch Spain's first general strike — demanding the right to form trade unions (asociaciones obreras) and the ten-hour working day; the military suppresses it; the strike leader Josep Barceló is executed; the event establishes the Barcelona working class as Spain's primary labour movement and documents the gap between liberal political rights and industrial social rights.
entries.note: The general strike 2 Jul 1855: documented in Arxiu Municipal Contemporani de Barcelona (AMCB) and in the military Captain General's dispatches (AGS). The execution of Barceló: documented in judicial records. Izard (1973), Industrialización y obrerismo: the standard study using factory and municipal records. Termes (1965), El movimiento obrero en España 1840–1900: the standard labour history. The demands (asociaciones and ten-hour day): documented in the workers' petition — AMCB. This is the direct precursor to the 1909 Semana Trágica documented in the 1900-1975 folio.

entries.label: Slavery abolished in Cuba (1886) — Spain's last enslaved population
entries.hint: Slavery is abolished in Cuba by royal decree on 7 October 1886 — making Spain one of the last colonial powers to end slavery in the Americas; the enslaved population of Cuba (estimated 200,000 in 1860) had already been declining through manumission and the patronato system (1880); the abolition comes after Puerto Rico (1873) and under intense international pressure.
entries.note: Royal decree abolishing slavery in Cuba 7 Oct 1886: Gaceta de Madrid — primary. The patronato system (transitional status, 1880–1886): established by Ley Moret 1870. Scott (1985), Slave Emancipation in Cuba: The Transition to Free Labor 1860–1899: the standard study using Cuban and Spanish archives. The Puerto Rico abolition 22 Mar 1873: documented. The international pressure (British anti-slavery societies): documented in Foreign Office records (National Archives, UK).

entries.label: Civil Code (1889) — women as legal minors under their husbands
entries.hint: Spain's Civil Code (1889) codifies women's legal subordination — a married woman requires her husband's permission (licencia marital) to sign contracts, open a bank account, work, travel or appear in court; this legal incapacity persists (with modifications) until Franco's partial reforms and is not fully abolished until the 1975 reform; the 1889 Code gives Arenal and feminist reformers their primary target.
entries.note: Código Civil 24 Jul 1889: Gaceta de Madrid — primary document. Article 60: 'The husband is the representative of his wife' — the foundational provision of female legal incapacity. Aresti (2001), Médicos, donjuanes y mujeres modernas: contextualises the Code's gender assumptions. Scanlon (1976), La polémica feminista en la España contemporánea 1868–1974: the standard study of feminist responses. The licencia marital: abolished only by Franco's reform of 1975 (Ley 14/1975) — the 86-year arc documented.

entries.label: Higinia Balaguer — the Fuencarral murder and Spain's first media trial
entries.loc: Madrid (Calle de Fuencarral; execution at the prison)
entries.hint: Higinia Balaguer, a domestic servant, is executed on 2 July 1890 for the murder of her mistress Luciana Borcino on the Calle de Fuencarral — the case generates Spain's first modern media trial; newspapers (El Liberal, El Imparcial) run daily coverage; feminist reformers including Concepción Arenal argue the verdict was driven by class prejudice; the case triggers a public debate about justice, class and the treatment of working-class women.
entries.note: The trial records: Archivo del Tribunal Supremo. Newspaper coverage: El Liberal and El Imparcial July–December 1888 and 1890 (BNE Hemeroteca) — the primary media record. Concepción Arenal's intervention: documented in her correspondence and in the press. Galdós fictionalises the case in La incógnita (1889): primary literary response. Sánchez Llama (2000), Galería de escritoras isabelinas: contextualises the feminist response. The case is Spain's equivalent of the Dreyfus Affair in miniature — a class-inflected miscarriage of justice generating permanent public debate.

Hundred Thousand Sons of Saint Louis

Third Carlist War — last Carlist stand
Ley Moyano — foundation of public education
Concordat — Church reconciled with liberal state
Bécquer: Rimas — Spanish Romanticism's defining voice
Gaudí takes Sagrada Família — Catalan Modernisme
Krausism — philosophical engine of Spanish liberalism
Pi i Margall — theory of Spanish federalism
Menéndez Pelayo — conservative Catholic intellectual Spain

Railway Law — wider gauge isolates Spain
Spain absent from Concert of Europe

Slavery abolished in Cuba — Spain's last
Civil Code — women as legal minors
